schools.summary: We are stewards, not lords, of goods we do not own (Pseudo-Chrysostom, Ambrose); to abuse the trust to our pleasures makes us guilty stewards; Bede places it after repentance, on almsgiving.
Given by Luke alone, to the disciples after the Pharisees' self-righteousness; Ellicott notes its many discordant interpretations.
Calvin: the leading object — deal generously with neighbours, that we may reap the fruit at the judgment; do not press every detail.
'Give an account' — produce the books; not directly the last judgment but a circumstance preparing the parable; 'how is it I hear this?' wonder and indignation (cf Isa 5:4).
Gill: God's call through the prophets, John, Christ, and the apostles.
He will not dig (too low) and is ashamed to beg, yet not ashamed of fraud; false shame preferring fraud to poverty, shuddering to sit like Lazarus.
Wesley: ashamed to beg but not to cheat — honour by men, pride by angels.
'I am resolved' a sudden solution ('I see into it now'); dishonesty leads to difficulty; the dramatic abruptness leaves 'they' to the sequel — he secures his future by his master's goods.

The debtors likely tenants paying rent in kind; in the interpretation, the 'debtors' are those toward whom we may show mercy (cf the Lord's Prayer) — the steward spends his master's goods to win friends.
The 'measure' the Hebrew bath (~7.5 gallons of olive oil, costly, 1 Kgs 5:11); 'thy bond' the written contract; 'quickly' — a secret arrangement needing speed; the debt halved.
The 'measure' the Hebrew cor/homer (~8-10 bushels of wheat); the reduction a fifth (vs the oil's half), reflecting relative values; the steward multiplies his future securities.
Augustine: not the fraud but the foresight to be imitated; we should never act deceitfully against our Lord even to give alms.
The 'lord' is the steward's master (not Christ), admiring the sharpness — commended for wisdom, not injustice; the world wiser in its affairs than the children of light in theirs.
Calvin/Gill: God cannot approve the injustice (contrary to his nature), only the craft in providing for the future.

debates.question: Why is the unjust steward commended?

sides.position: for his prudence and foresight, not his dishonesty

schools.summary: Gregory: place riches in the poor's hands before death; Theophylact: the 'riches of unrighteousness' are goods meant for our brethren spent on self; Cyril: love the friendship of the poor, treasure in heaven.
Money ('mammon of unrighteousness') to be used discreetly for the kingdom; proper to make friends by it; 'when ye fail' = at death.
Calvin: not seeking human advocates but, by charity, favour with God (Ps 18:25) — not merit but rewarded faith; Wesley: do all possible good, especially to God's children.
A tested rule: fidelity (or fraud) in the least reveals and forms the character for the much; 'one of the profoundest sayings'; money is the 'least,' grace the 'true riches.'

The 'true riches' (peace, pardon, the unsearchable riches of Christ, Eph 3:8) set against the deceitful mammon; misuse of money forfeits grace; Robertson's warning to preachers caught in money's snare.
Earthly wealth is 'another's' — God's, lent as a trust, withdrawn at death; the 'your own' the true, abiding inheritance; we are stewards not possessors.
Calvin: the 'your own' is the heavenly riches within; God gives wealth not that we cling to it; Wesley: God is proprietor of all, we only stewards.
Same as Matt 6:24 (with 'household servant'); the heart wholly God's or wholly the world's (Josh 24:15); an axiom Christ loved to repeat.
Henry: the covetous Pharisees treated it with contempt — the response the next verse records.
'Lovers of money' (philargyroi, cf 2 Tim 3:2); the imperfect 'scoffed' (turned up the nose), having listened all the while; they contemned the doctrine that condemned their love of money.
Bede: they derided the Preacher of mercy, humility, and frugality.
Calvin: Christ does not yield to their mockery but maintains his doctrine, a pattern for ministers facing despisers.
They justify themselves before men, but God knows hearts (Jer 17:10); 'abomination' (bdelygma) a withering word; linked to the Pharisee of Luke 18:9.
Calvin: their hypocrisy before men will be exposed before God; Wesley: pride feeds covetousness, derides the gospel, destroys the law.

themes: esteemed of men, abomination to God

schools.summary: Set beside Matt 11:12-13; 'presseth' (biazetai) — either storming the kingdom by zeal (Beza) or violence against it (Vulgate); the Pharisees ignored that all revelation led up to John.
Calvin: grace now seen openly, not in dark shadows; Wesley: the humble receive it with inexpressible earnestness.

debates.question: Does 'every man presseth into it' mean eager reception or violent opposition?

sides.position: the eager, forceful zeal that lays hold of the kingdom
the violence done against the kingdom

schools.summary: Referring to Matt 5:18; lest the law seem abrogated with 'until John,' Christ affirms it cannot fail (cf Ps 102:26; Isa 40:8); the gospel upholds, not abolishes, the law.
Gill: the law not laid aside; heaven and earth would sooner pass than it fail.
A chosen instance of the law's permanence — the Pharisees lowered marriage's sacredness by easy divorce; Christ presses its intent (fuller at Matt 5:32; 19:9).
Tertullian (anti-Marcion): Christ forbids divorce, restoring the intent of Moses' permitted repudiation (Deut 24:1), not contradicting the law.
Luxury disgraces riches (Ambrose); the rich man unnamed, the poor named, for God approves the humble not the proud (Gregory); condemned not for fraud but selfish luxury amid want (Bede).
The costly purple and fine linen of the wealthy; the sin is the daily feasting while a beggar starved; Henry: one may have the world's wealth yet perish.
Gill/Calvin: a parable, not a particular history; Lightfoot mocks the friars who point to the rich man's house.

debates.question: Is the rich man and Lazarus a parable or a real history?

sides.position: a parable teaching what may happen

schools.summary: 'Lazarus' (Hebrew 'God a help' / destitute of help) suits his condition; 'was laid' (had been flung there) with contemptuous roughness; the one named figure in the parables — named for his good character and glorious end.
He craved the crumbs (cf the prodigal, Luke 15:16; the Syro-Phoenician, Mark 7:28); the dogs, more merciful, licked his sores — probably he got the scraps, nothing more.
Calvin: a drop of humanity would have sent food from the kitchen; he did not learn pity even from the dogs.
The one received by angels, the other by torments (Pseudo-Chrysostom); God's judgment a mirror of our dispositions (Gregory of Nyssa); Augustine: 'Abraham's bosom' not corporeal.
'Abraham's bosom' the popular Jewish image of the blessed feast; the angels bear the righteous (taken literally, as the burial); the estates reversed at death.
The word is Hades (the unseen world), not Gehenna — an earlier stage; 'in torments' (basanois, the rack); the lost far off from the righteous, the sight sharpening the pain.
Calvin: spiritual things under figures adapted to our senses — souls have no bodily eyes, but the parable paints the real state of the life to come.
He clings to 'Father Abraham' (Jewish pride) and still treats Lazarus as a servant to be sent; begs the least mercy — a finger-tip of water — having denied the beggar crumbs.
Wesley: this lone instance of praying to a departed saint is no warrant — it is a damned soul praying, and in vain.
'Son' not disowned, yet a cutting word; 'Remember' itself part of the torment; 'receivedst in full' thy chosen good things; condemned for making earthly good his portion.
Wesley: he chose worldly things as his happiness — no wonder he was in torments.
The 'gulf' fixed, firm, immovable (esteriktai, 'there on purpose'); it forever divides heaven and hell, like a scene from Dante.
Calvin: the permanency of the future state — the boundaries never broken through; return early to the path, lest we fall headlong into the abyss.

Gregory: hopeless for himself, he turns to his brethren; his knowledge and memory preserved, deepening torment; Augustine: he might wish it without knowing their state; Chrysostom: their care is God's, who gave Moses and the prophets.
Robertson: the Catholic appeal to this for prayer to saints fails — both are in Hades, and Abraham makes no effort to reach the brothers.
He deludes himself that his brothers will believe a man from the dead; the motive weighed — selfish fear or a first unselfish anxiety (Ellicott inclines to the latter).
'Moses and the prophets' = the OT, to be heard and read; they had them but let them not influence their lives; the right use of lower knowledge the condition of the higher (cf John 5:39).
Calvin: we have an undoubted rule of life in Scripture, God's appointed and sufficient witness — no right to expect the dead to rise and instruct us.
He urges the novelty of one from the dead; now sees repentance the one thing needful (he had thought it for others); the Jewish craving for a 'sign' (1 Cor 1:22).
Calvin: it voices the feelings of the living — the perpetual demand for wonders (angels, the dead) in place of the word.

The heart that resists Scripture would resist a risen dead; fulfilled when the raising of Lazarus (John 11:43) hardened the Pharisees and Christ's own resurrection left the unbelieving unbelieving; a discouragement to seeking the dead.
Wesley: repentance is an entire change of heart — a thousand apparitions cannot effect it; God only, applying his word.
